title: Workbook — The Our Father
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. Why did Tertullian call the Our Father «the summary of the whole Gospel»?
  2. What is the structure of the Our Father (invocation plus petitions)?
  3. What does the Aramaic word Abba mean and why is it important?
  4. Why do we say «Our Father» and not «My Father»?
  5. When we say «who art in heaven,» are we talking about a place?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "right" answers to these personal questions.

1. What is your personal experience with the Our Father? Do you pray it attentively or on autopilot?

Many Catholics have said this prayer thousands of times. What does it truly mean to you?

 

 

2. When you say «Our Father,» who are you including as brothers and sisters?

Think of specific people: your family, your parish, even those who have hurt you. Is there anyone you struggle to include?

 

 

3. How does your experience with your earthly father influence the way you relate to God the Father?

Whether it was a good experience or a difficult one, our human image of fatherhood can affect our relationship with God. Explore this honestly.

 

 

4. Do you pray to God with filial trust or with fear? How did you learn to pray as a child?

Sometimes we were raised with an image of God closer to judge than to Father. Does anything like that happen to you?

 

 

5. If Jesus asked you today «do you want me to teach you to pray?», what would you ask him to teach you?

Like the first disciples, be humble. What is missing in your prayer life?

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Pray the Our Father Word by Word

Over five days, take a different phrase of the Our Father each day and meditate on it for five minutes. Write down what you discover. Day 1: «Our Father.» Day 2: «Who art in heaven.» Day 3: «Hallowed be thy name.» Day 4: «Thy kingdom come.» Day 5: «Thy will be done.» Write your reflections:

 

 

 

 

 

B. Write Your Own Version of the Our Father

Imagine you are one of the disciples on the mountain. You have heard Jesus teach you this prayer. Now translate it into your own language, without losing the meaning. How would you express each petition? Write it:

 

 

 

 

C. Include Someone in Your "Our Father"

Think of someone with whom you have difficulties in your relationship. This week, when you pray the Our Father, consciously think of that person when you say «our» and «forgive us.» What changed in you after doing this for several days?

 

 

 

Scripture to Memorize

«You have received a spirit of adoption as sons, by which we cry, 'Abba! Father!'»

— Romans 8:15

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, you who taught me to call God «Abba,»
teach me to pray as you pray,
with the trust of a child,
with the humility of one who knows that all is gift.
That in saying «Our Father»
I may not forget my brothers and sisters,
and in saying «forgive us»
I may not forget to forgive.
That every Our Father may be a step
toward the heaven where you await me.
Amen.

Print Workbook
